Scenario.title: FunnyDream Campaign: Healthcare Infrastructure Compromise
Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting healthcare organizations, leveraging phishing, unpatched software, and credential theft to encrypt critical systems and demand ransom.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Target Artifact (Tasks), File System, Internet-facing assets, Application Log, Windows Registry, Logon Session, OS Process, Target Artifact (Accessibility), Credential Store, File, Process, Target Artifact (Key)
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Malicious Shell Modification, Delivery Method, Application Log Content (Mechanism), Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Keylogging, Reg.exe Utility, Pass the Hash, File Modification (Mechanism), Process Creation (Mechanism), Data Encrypted for Impact

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker uses artificial intelligence tools to analyze publicly available information about healthcare providers, identifying vulnerable systems and staff members likely to engage with phishing content.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The group develops custom malware by modifying legitimate shell scripts to evade signature-based detection, preparing payloads for later deployment.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2023-37580 (Suggested): Synacor Zimbra Collaboration Suite (ZCS) contains a cross-site scripting vulnerability
stage.description: The attackers send spear-phishing emails disguised as medical supply orders, embedding malicious links that exploit unpatched Zimbra email servers to establish initial access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2020-3952 (Suggested): VMware vCenter Server information disclosure vulnerability
stage.description: Exploiting a misconfigured VMware vCenter Server, the attackers inject malicious commands into application logs to execute arbitrary code and gain control of the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A scheduled task is created in the Windows Registry to maintain persistent access, ensuring the malware runs automatically upon system reboot.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-70: Try Common or Default Usernames and Passwords
stage.description: The attackers leverage weak administrative credentials, exploiting default logon sessions to escalate privileges and access restricted resources.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legitimate processes to hide its presence from security monitoring tools, evading detection mechanisms.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: A keylogger is deployed to capture administrator credentials from infected workstations, enabling further lateral movement.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: The attackers use the Reg.exe utility to sniff network traffic and map internal network segments, identifying additional hosts to compromise.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Using stolen credentials, the attackers move laterally across the network via Pass the Hash techniques to compromise additional systems.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Sensitive patient records and operational data are exfiltrated and stored locally before being encrypted to maximize extortion value.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Data is exfiltrated using process creation tunnels to obscure network traffic patterns, avoiding traditional firewall rules.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Critical systems are encrypted using AES-256, forcing the victim to pay a ransom to regain access to patient data and operational systems.

Scenario.summary: The FunnyDream campaign demonstrates a coordinated attack on healthcare infrastructure, combining reconnaissance, phishing, and ransomware to disrupt operations and extort funds. The attackers exploit both human and technical weaknesses to achieve full system control.
